appliedJudgementItems.item: Take task back when team struggles (reverse-coded)

assessmentQuestions.text: Empowerment means:
A manager demonstrates the right mindset by:
How confident are you in empowering your team and building ownership?
What is key to an effective leadership conversation?
Effective feedback should primarily focus on:
How confident are you in handling difficult or performance-related conversations?
Effective prioritization is based on:
Delegation is effective when:
How confident are you in delegating tasks effectively?
What is the most effective way to ensure team alignment with business goals?
Which of the following best drives execution consistency?
How confident do you feel in creating clear alignment and momentum within your team?
What is the most effective way to influence stakeholders?
Stakeholder mapping helps in:
How confident are you in influencing stakeholders without authority?
When priorities are unclear, I ensure alignment before execution begins.
When faced with a difficult conversation, I prepare and address it proactively.
When stakeholders resist, I try to understand their perspective before responding.
When my team struggles with a task, I tend to take it back and complete it myself. (reverse-coded)
I actively create opportunities for my team to take ownership and make decisions.

High Effectiveness.description: Level 3. Demonstrates strong knowledge, confidence, and applied judgement across modules.

Developing.description: Level 2. Mid-range across knowledge / confidence / behaviors with focused growth areas.

Emerging.description: Level 1. Foundational stage; needs structured development across modules.

learnerData.situation: One of my team member was falling behind. Based on the internal discussion I had to communicate very clear expectation in coming quarters via official feedback and during the catch-up. learnerData.situation: I gave 3 people to complete a task. As they were relatively new in the firm, each one of them thought other person will do it and the task was skipped. I would have clearly explained that all three are accountable for completion on this task and try to divide things among themselves. If they were not able to divide, i would have offered my assistance to divide the tasks.

learnerData.strengths:
1. Empowering My Team - I focus on understanding each person's strengths and give them meaningful ownership of their work, which drives engagement and growth.

2. Providing Clear Direction - I align my team on goals and priorities early, especially in ambiguous situations, so we stay focused and move forward with confidence.
learnerData.challenges:
1. Managing Competing Priorities Keeping the team aligned when stakeholder priorities shift frequently — managed through clear communication and regular realignment.

2. Balancing Growth & Delivery Investing in team development without compromising on delivery timelines — addressed by embedding growth into everyday work.
learnerData.situation: Early on, I delayed giving direct feedback to an underperforming team member, hoping the issues would self-correct. Instead, it impacted team morale and delivery. I would address performance concerns early and directly, with clear expectations and a structured support plan — rather than waiting for the situation to escalate.

learnerData.name: Mahima Khetarpal
learnerData.strengths:
1. Approachable
2. Strategic thinker
learnerData.challenges:
1. Providing constructive feedback
2. Delegation
learnerData.situation:
I once assigned a task to a team member that was submitted without completing the required basic checks. At the time, I corrected the issues myself during the review process to ensure the client deliverable was submitted on time. However, I later realized that while the immediate issue was resolved, I missed the opportunity to provide detailed constructive feedback and ensure proper ownership from the team member.
Looking back, I would have spent more time discussing the gaps, explaining the expected quality standards, and identifying whether there was a knowledge or training gap. Since then, I have focused more on coaching team members, providing actionable feedback, and ensuring clarity of expectations to improve accountability and overall team performance.

learnerData.strengths:
1. Leading by example in terms of professional behavior
2. Listening to different perspectives without any prejudice
learnerData.challenges:
1. Managing folks where I'm their line manager but their allocations are aligned with another manager
2. When a low performer who was under another manager is realigned to me, I feel it's difficult to gauge them sometimes
learnerData.situation:
A senior analyst, M, was technically brilliant but resisted our team's migration from Excel to a new BI tool, claiming it was "less accurate." Instead of fully switching, he kept working in Excel and manually transferring data into the new system — doubling his workload and slowing down the entire team's closing process.

As his manager, I avoided confronting him directly due to his high-performer status, resulting in me essentially managing two parallel reporting systems.

What I'd Do Differently
*Data-driven feedback — Show M the exact delays and error rates his workaround caused
* Empathize first — Ask why the new tool made him uncomfortable, addressing the root cause
* Give him ownership — Make him the "Process Champion" to identify and fix gaps in the BI tool
* Set a hard deadline — Sunset the Excel model with a firm timeline, framing adoption as a performance expectation, not a suggestion

learnerData.name: Satya Siva Venkatesh Ganni
learnerData.strengths:
1. Empower team to take ownership and deliver the objectives effectively
2. Give constructive feedback for performance improvement
learnerData.challenges:
1. In a multi location setup, it gets difficult to coordinate and do effective resource utilization.
2. Dealing with newer resources is a bit challenging at times
learnerData.situation: NA

learnerKeyInferences.title: Inference 1 — Knowledge baseline is solid across the cohort
learnerKeyInferences.body: Section 2 cohort average is 47.2/50 (≈94%). All five modules cluster between 4.48 and 5 on a /5 scale — no module is structurally weak. Learners arrive with a credible managerial knowledge base; the programme is reinforcing, not remediating.

learnerKeyInferences.title: Inference 2 — Managing Priorities & Delegation is the strongest knowledge module
learnerKeyInferences.body: Managing Priorities & Delegation leads at 5/5 — learners clearly understand its core principles. Confidence on this module, however, is the lowest at 4.13/5 (Knowledge→Confidence gap of -0.87). They know what to do; they do not yet feel ready to do it under pressure.

learnerKeyInferences.title: Inference 3 — Applied judgement trails knowledge
learnerKeyInferences.body: Section 3 cohort average is 20.4/25 (≈81%). The behaviours that translate knowledge into action lag the knowledge score itself — typical of pre-program cohorts where the model is understood faster than it is practised.

learnerKeyInferences.title: Inference 4 — Q4 reverse-coded flag
learnerKeyInferences.body: 50% of the cohort agreed with the reverse-coded statement on taking work back when the team struggles. That is the single most visible self-reported development opportunity.

learnerNarrativePatterns.title: Self-Correction & Applied Learning
learnerNarrativePatterns.pattern: Learner not only identifies what went wrong but articulates a specific behaviour they have since changed or committed to changing. The reflection moves beyond diagnosis — it describes a concrete corrective action already applied or clearly planned.
learnerNarrativePatterns.implication: These learners have already begun the doer-to-leader shift. The programme should channel this self-awareness into structured practice — they are ready for higher-complexity scenarios and peer coaching roles within the cohort.

learnerNarrativePatterns.title: Delayed Intervention
learnerNarrativePatterns.pattern: Learner describes a situation where they waited too long to act — on feedback, ownership clarity, a difficult conversation, or a boundary that needed to be set. In each case, the delay allowed the situation to escalate or the team to develop a gap that was harder to close later.
learnerNarrativePatterns.implication: This is not a knowledge gap — these learners can articulate what they should have done. It is a behavioural readiness gap. Scenarios and role-plays that create low-stakes practice for early intervention will be most effective.

managerCohortSummary.assessmentTitle: New Managers — Pre Program Manager of Learner Assessment

managerImprovements.label: Delegation — assigning and truly letting go

managerKeyInferences.title: Inference 1 — Domain strength is visible and consistent
managerKeyInferences.body: Across 105 behaviour ratings (7 managers × 5 modules × 3 items), zero "Rarely" ratings were given. The cohort is perceived as operating between "Sometimes" and "Often" — a solid foundation for a first-time manager cohort.

managerKeyInferences.title: Inference 2 — Influence Without Authority is the standout strength
managerKeyInferences.body: Influence Without Authority is the highest observed module at 8/10 — managers see these learners as credible and effective in this area, even at this early stage of their managerial tenure.

managerKeyInferences.title: Inference 3 — Creating Constant Momentum is the lowest-observed module
managerKeyInferences.body: Behaviours associated with Creating Constant Momentum sit at 6.5/10. Managers see room for tightening these practices through the programme.

managerKeyInferences.title: Inference 4 — Section 3 reverse-coded item
managerKeyInferences.body: On the reverse-coded behaviour (taking back tasks when the team struggles), the cohort averages 2/5 — managers themselves flag this as the most uneven behaviour, mirroring the learner self-view.

managerLearnerData.improvements: Driving collective goals, mentoring, influencing/motivating others.

While on a meeting with a client, Richa appeared bit shaky and was not able to clearly give the required guidance to client on their specific query. She could have had meetings within the team first to align properly before going into the client meeting

managerLearnerData.strengths: He is very approachable to his team which makes them comfortable around him. He has a strategic thought process which helps him identify areas of manual intervention and work with the team to overcome the same.
managerLearnerData.improvements: He should work on providing constructive feedback to his team and also delegate+follow up on action items intermittently.
managerLearnerData.situation: In cases where the team has missed an important task instead of trying to look for avenues of getting out of it, take ownership and define next steps to overcome such issues from recurring.

managerLearnerData.strengths:
He has a positive attitude and keeps the overall delivery in mind - for e.g., there were setup issues by the onboarding team that were not captured during the handover of a recently onboarded client. He got his hands dirty in the interest of the overall delivery and ensured a smooth execution.
He is good with bringing in process efficiencies in the team through automation. This helps reduce time spent by team on tasks.
managerLearnerData.improvements:
Fahad can work on structuring his communication (client and with internal senior stakeholders) more effectively. This involves having a clear agenda before calls, structuring and putting forward his thought process in a clear manner and have unambiguous takeaways / action items for respective teams.
Fahad should delegate to team members more - this will ensure multiple people in the team have the required knowledge to take up tasks and ensure his bandwidth is more effectively utilized.
managerLearnerData.situation: Driving a conversation on one of his clients on a new setup for physical collateral blotters. This required getting a clear understanding of requirement with client and broker and documenting next steps and action items with stakeholders. It also required communicating project status and delays internally in a proactive manner.

managerSection2Overall.narrative: No manager gave a rating of 1 (Rarely) across any item in any module. The cohort is perceived as between "Sometimes" and "Often" overall — with Managing Priorities & Delegation and Empowerment & Managerial Mindset showing the strongest "Consistently" clusters (33.3% and 23.8%), and Impactful Leadership Conversations showing the weakest cluster (9.5% Consistently).

I would have clearly explained that all three are accountable for completion on this task and try to divide things among themselves. sections.title: Managing Self
sections.description: Empowerment, managerial mindset, and self-driven ownership.

sections.title: Managing Teams
sections.description: Leading conversations, feedback, prioritization, and delegation.

sections.title: Managing Business
sections.description: Driving alignment, momentum, and influencing stakeholders.

teamSizeNarrative: 1-3 group consistently outscores larger-team groups across all modules — particularly Influence Without Authority (+0.39 vs 4-6, +0.13 vs 7-10). The 7-10 group's uniform 4.4 across all modules may reflect either genuine uniformity or a conservative self-rating approach under higher team complexity.
